title: Should the Fed hike rates in 2026?

Happy Thursday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale,
presenting our macro minute for
Thursday, July 2nd, 2026. Jobs report
Thursday, so that's exciting. As always,
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's lead-off morning notes, so
let's dive right in. Today's key macro
question is, should the Fed hike rates
in 2026? The short answer is no. As we
have repeatedly stressed in our
research, the current build-up of core
inflationary pressures in the US economy
will likely be largely unfazed by any
modest tightening of the policy rate
tool. The Fed must use its balance sheet
to rein in public and private sector
driven excesses that are supporting
income, wealth, consumption, and
investment among the households,
businesses, and sectors that risk
becoming permanently perched atop the
K-shaped US economy because of
state-sponsored AI diffusion.
In terms of analytical nuance, the June
jobs report, June auto sales, and the
latest initial and continuing jobless
claims data generally supported the
consensus outlook for slowing growth.
The June private sector labor income
data did not support our resilient US
economy or paradigm I'm C A K run it hot
themes. The June temporary employment
and cyclical unemployment data supported
our resilient US economy and and and
paradigm C run it hot themes. The June
private sector employment and private
sector average weekly hours data did not
support our resilient US economy and
paradigm C themes. Declining below trend
readings in the U3 headline unemployment
rate and U6 underemployment rate signal
a tight labor market that is getting
tighter from a cyclical perspective,
supporting our resilient US economy
paradigm C A K run it hot and sticky
inflation themes in the process. The
declining well above trend reading in
long-term unemployed as a percent of
total signals a lack of dynamism from a
structural perspective, which supports
our jobless recovery theme. Recall that
this time series bottomed at 17.8% in
February of 2023. The latest reading of
27.3 is concerning in this context.
For what it's worth, uh the U3 headline
unemployment rate would have been a well
above trend 5.5%
in June if the BLS adjusted for the
ongoing cyclical and structural decline
in labor force engagement among prime
working age workers.
Elsewhere, the June auto sales data
supported our resilient US economy and
Paradigm CA run of hot themes. Initial
and continuing jobless claims could
signal a middling probability of
developing recession in the US economy.
All the 42 macro fat five recession
signaling indicators in the 42 macro
business cycle model signal a low
probability of developing recession in
the US economy. In fact, the latter
currently signals the US business cycle
is heating up with nearly all the
interconnected cycles that comprise it
in an accelerating upturn, i.e.
featuring growth rates that are above
trend and increasing.
The June jobs report supported the
consensus outlook for peaking inflation.
The June private sector average hourly
earnings data supported our sticky
inflation and cooling housing and labor
themes. The June civilian labor force
data did not support our cooling housing
and labor theme. It did, however,
support our sticky inflation theme. The
minus 2.5% 6-month annualized growth
rate of labor supply compares to a 19.6%
6-month annualized growth rate of labor
demand, according to the latest private
sector job openings data.
Divergent signals from low productivity
labor demand and high productivity labor
demand make it difficult to assess
whether hawks or doves currently have a
better beat on the labor market.
Divergent signals from high income real
wage growth and low income real wage
growth make it difficult to assess
whether hawks or doves currently have a
better read on the labor market as well.
As is tradition, the revisions to to the
prior month's nonfarm payroll gains were
awful. This is supportive of our view
that Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's data task
force and the productivity and jobs task
force will contribute to a marginally
dovish interpretation of the labor
market, trend productivity, and trend
inflation via the infusion of
alternative near real-time data. Post
the June jobs report, US dollar money
markets are now pricing in 24 basis
points of tightening over the next 12
months, down seven basis points day over
day. Post the June jobs report, the 42
macro market implied Fed neutral rate
model still sees the Fed current Fed's
current policy setting as modestly
accommodative. It must hike one to two
times to get back to neutral.
Elsewhere, OpenAI discussing a US
government stake with CEO Sam Altman
proposing 5%. This would open the door
for US federal government to purchase at
least 5% stakes in the other top LLM
developers, including Anthropic, Google,
and Meta Platforms. The stake
The stake concept coincides with the
Trump administration's historic merging
of private and public sector interests.
Critics deride this strategy for the
morality of the government picking
winners and losers, as well as the
structural distortions it may cause in
the economy and mispricings in asset
markets, especially at this early stage
of AI diffusion. Proponents deride this
strategy for the adverse Sorry,
proponents support the strategy for the
adverse national security implications
of leaving exclusive control of AI
development and diffusion in private
sector hands. Think the Manhattan
Project. What if we did that with
Manhattan Project? As always, the
nuanced truth rests somewhere in between
these hyperpartisan views. As always,
I'll wrap up with a question from our
community.
This one's titled question on the on the
question of forward guidance. This
reads, "Kevin Warsh's
debut debut was refreshingly blunt. No
more forecasts, no forward guidance,
just the plain prices are too high after
years of parsing every comma in a Powell
statement. A central banker who just
says the thing sounds great, though it's
early. Talk is easy in July. He's not
just changing the top five task forces,
suggests he's remodeling the whole
house. And with everyone begging him to
quote buy the
to buy the AI will crush inflation cut
rate story, he passed and kept inflation
first. It helps he's not alone. He and
Treasury Secretary Yellen, two
Druckenmiller proteges, share one
worldview, and holding rates now is what
can make later cuts look earned, not
forced. The initial read is buying it.
Yields up dollar firm on September hike
now live. Counterintuitively, that
credible Treasury Fed line may be
bullish until it looks like the Fed is
just monetizing deficits. Big if, so
call me an optimist. We all wait and
see. So, love this. Uh really not a
question, but I I I wanted to highlight
this because it's consistent with what
we've been saying, which is the Fed must
play-action pass so that it can run the
ball later. Play-action pass means you
need to tighten now so that you can back
the linebackers and the safeties off
from a bond market concerning about
inflation perspective so that you can
hammer the run later in the game with
more e-e-e- more easing, more dovish
easing. Recall that we're we're on the
uh tape uh you know, well out in front
of Global Wall Street on this and that
the the net result of the five task
forces will be dovish. Communication
will be hawkish. Balance sheet will be
marginally hawkish and much less hawkish
than what's currently priced in,
particularly when you look at the gold
and Bitcoin markets. And then you look
at the data task force, that's going to
be unequivocally dovish the net in terms
of the the marginal interpretation on
monetary policy. Uh that's going to the
productivity and jobs task force will be
unequivocally dovish as well as the
inflation task force will be
unequivocally dovish. So, refer to our
uh June 20th Around the Horn uh
presentation for more details on all
that. So, we'll wrap it up there.

Independence Day uh long weekend. A

and and what it represents. Obviously,
we had some some some dark forces, you
know, perpetuating K-shaped regulatory
policy, K-shaped monetary policy,

criminal justice policy in this country,
but those aren't permanent features of
America. I think the founding fathers
got it right and I think we have an
opportunity here to get it right in this
war turning if we if we if we play our
cards right and and and band together,
unite unite as one people as opposed to,
